coredata.dc:title: Full-field experimental characterization of charging-induced swelling in lithium-ion pouch cells using surround digital image correlation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Pirker, Christoph
Mohr, Dirk
Tancogne-Dejean, Thomas
coredata.dc:description: Lithium plating is a major degradation mechanism in lithium-ion cells influenced by local pressure, rate- and state-of-charge. In this work, heterogeneous plating in 4 Ah graphite/LiCoO2 pouch cells is investigated using surround three-dimensional digital image correlation (DIC), enabling full-field measurements of in-plane strain and thickness evolution on both faces during charging. Cells are charged using CC–CV protocols at 0.5C, 1.0C, and 1.5C under unconstrained conditions and constant compressive loads of 0.026 MPa and 1 MPa. Full-field thickness maps reveal an early onset of heterogeneous swelling with increasing charge rate, showing strain localization at cell edges and center. The maximum swelling overshoots during the CV/rest stages followed by partial relaxation, consistent with plating and stripping, whereas a plateau region of low-variance strain accompanies intercalation-dominated swelling. At 1.5C, strain relaxation is observed over essentially the full surface, indicating widespread plating, while most of the surface remain within the plateau regime at lower charge rates. Applying ∼1 MPa pressure reduces the plating-associated contribution, yielding thickness evolution close to the plateau strain at low charge rate. These results demonstrate that surround DIC provides a direct, high-throughput route to spatially resolve lithium plating in pouch cells and quantify how mechanical constraint mitigates plating-induced swelling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Pouch cell
Digital image correlation
Swelling
Plating

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Unconstrained charging with surround DIC analysis

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Pouch cell architecture and (b) view in the surround DIC setup. (c) Surround DIC setup with ① the cell suspended vertically from two alligator clips and rest on a support ②. ③ and ④, camera systems facing the opposite large sides of the pouch cell, ⑤ blue anti-glare lighting and ⑥ charging device. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The strain maps of the cells, for various C-rates, (a-c) 0.5C, (d) 1.0C and (e) 1.5C. The displayed images are taken in the initial state (with thickness displayed) and at increasing State of Charge (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, end of charge, with thickness strain displayed). Note that the cell terminals are located at the top of the cell.

Strain evolution along the horizontal (left) and vertical (right) axis. (a-b) C-rate of 0.5C, (c-d) C-rate of 1.0C and (e-f) C-rate of 1.5C. The lines are equally spaced every 0.1 SoC except for the last one which corresponds to the end of charge.

Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution observed using surround DIC. Charging is presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. All strains including minimum, maximum and plateau strains. The end of CC, and end of CV (end of charge) are reported with cross (dashed line) and circle (full line).

Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution observed using surround DIC. Charging is presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. All strains including minimum, maximum and plateau strains. The end of CC, and end of CV (end of charge) are reported with cross (dashed line) and circle (full line).

Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution upon charging with 4 kN of compression. Charging is presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. The crosses denote the change from CC to CV while the circles indicate the end of charge. The charging current is shown in grey.

Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution upon charging with 0.1 kN of compression. Charging is presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. The crosses denote the change from CC to CV while the circles indicate the end of charge. The charging current is shown in grey.

Comparison of swelling behavior for all tests at various C-rates (a-b) 0.5C, (c-d) 1.0C and (e-f) 1.5C. The left column shows the voltage evolution with state of charge while the right column shows the strain evolution. The crosses denote the change from CC to CV while the circles indicate the end of charge. The charging current is shown in grey.
Cell specifications (dimensions are measured in a fully discharged state).
Hardware parameters for the surround DIC system.
Analysis parameters for the surround DIC system.

head.ce:title: Full-field experimental characterization of charging-induced swelling in lithium-ion pouch cells using surround digital image correlation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH Zurich, Switzerland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH Zurich, Switzerland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium plating is a major degradation mechanism in lithium-ion cells influenced by local pressure, rate- and state-of-charge. In this work, heterogeneous plating in 4 Ah graphite/LiCoO2 pouch cells is investigated using surround three-dimensional digital image correlation (DIC), enabling full-field measurements of in-plane strain and thickness evolution on both faces during charging. Cells are charged using CC–CV protocols at 0.5C, 1.0C, and 1.5C under unconstrained conditions and constant compressive loads of 0.026 MPa and 1 MPa. Full-field thickness maps reveal an early onset of heterogeneous swelling with increasing charge rate, showing strain localization at cell edges and center. The maximum swelling overshoots during the CV/rest stages followed by partial relaxation, consistent with plating and stripping, whereas a plateau region of low-variance strain accompanies intercalation-dominated swelling. At 1.5C, strain relaxation is observed over essentially the full surface, indicating widespread plating, while most of the surface remain within the plateau regime at lower charge rates. Applying ∼1 MPa pressure reduces the plating-associated contribution, yielding thickness evolution close to the plateau strain at low charge rate. These results demonstrate that surround DIC provides a direct, high-throughput route to spatially resolve lithium plating in pouch cells and quantify how mechanical constraint mitigates plating-induced swelling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid expansion of electric vehicles, stationary energy storage, and portable electronics has intensified the demand for lithium-ion batteries with higher energy density, faster charging capability, and improved durability [1–3]. Among the various form factors, lithium-ion pouch cells are widely adopted due to their lightweight construction, high packaging efficiency, and geometric flexibility. However, their soft polymer–aluminum casing and layered jelly-roll architecture make pouch cells particularly sensitive to mechanically induced degradation mechanisms. Lithium-ion batteries exhibit a strong coupling between electrochemical processes and mechanical deformation. During cycling, reversible thickness changes arise from lithium intercalation and deintercalation in the electrodes, while irreversible contributions originate from side reactions such as solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, gas generation, and lithium plating [4–8]. When pouch cells are operated under confined conditions, these volumetric changes lead to local pressure build-up, which can further exacerbate degradation and, in extreme cases, induce internal short circuits.

Out-of-plane swelling is a particularly critical issue for pouch cells. Numerous experimental studies have investigated swelling behavior under varying rates of charge (C-rate), temperatures, and mechanical boundary conditions [9–12]. Both constant-force [13] and constant-displacement [14,15] loading configurations have been explored, and specialized fixtures have been developed to ensure uniform pressure application [16]. In most cases, swelling is monitored using single-point displacement measurements, such as linear variable differential transformers (LVDTs). While these approaches provide valuable average thickness information, they inherently lack spatial resolution and therefore cannot resolve localized swelling phenomena.

At high C-rates and low temperatures, lithium plating becomes one of the dominant degradation mechanisms [17,18]. Plating is strongly influenced by local current density, state-of-charge (SoC) heterogeneity, and mechanical pressure. Experimentally, lithium plating is commonly inferred from voltage relaxation behavior [19,20], thickness overshoot measurements [21,22], or stress evolution [14,15]. Due to electrode overhang and current distribution effects, plating often initiates near the edges of pouch cells [15,23]. However, the spatial extent and temporal evolution of plating-induced swelling remain difficult to quantify with conventional measurement techniques.

Recent advances in full-field optical methods enable non-contact measurement of deformation with high spatial resolution. Three-dimensional Digital Image Correlation (3D-DIC) has been applied to monitor battery swelling during charging and discharging [24–27], while alternative approaches based on laser profilometry have been proposed to map thickness evolution in an Eulerian framework [28–31]. Additionally, computed-tomography coupled with Digital Volume Correlation has been used to resolve strain and displacement within the bulk of the cell [32–34]. For example, Eastwood et al. [32] reported dilation maps during discharge of a coin cell with Li/Al metal anode and Manganese oxide cathode and observed larger swelling at the interface between the current collector and the cathode. Nevertheless, applications of full-field methods to quantify heterogeneous lithium plating remain scarce.

In this work, a multi-system 3D-DIC configuration, referred to as surround DIC [35], is employed to characterize the spatial and temporal thickness evolution of commercial lithium-ion pouch cells during charging. Surround DIC enables simultaneous measurement of both large faces of the cell in a Lagrangian reference frame, providing access to full-field thickness and in-plane strain distributions. Unconstrained charging experiments are performed at multiple C-rates to identify the onset and extent of heterogeneous swelling associated with lithium plating. The same charging protocols are subsequently repeated under constant compressive loads to assess the influence of external pressure on plating-induced swelling. By comparing unconstrained full-field measurements with constrained single-point thickness measurements, this study reveals (i) the spatially heterogeneous nature of lithium plating, (ii) the existence of a plateau swelling regime representative of intercalation-dominated behavior, and (iii) an additive decomposition of swelling strain into state-of-charge-dependent and rate-of-charge-dependent contributions under constrained conditions.

Commercially available 4 Ah capacity lithium-ion pouch cells with graphite/LCO chemistry are considered in this study. The relevant datasheet information is reported in Table 1 . Each pouch cell comprises a jelly stack and an aluminum-polymer casing. The jelly stack consists of repeating anode/separator/cathode assembly stacked with a meandering separator (see Fig. 1 a). Overall, the jelly-stack includes 17 double-sided graphite-coated copper anode layers (with total thickness of 0.145 mm), 16 double-sided aluminum cathode layers (0.12 mm thick), and two single-sided aluminum cathode layers (0.08 mm thick) at both ends of the stack. The copper and aluminum current collectors are about 7 μm thick and 12 μm thick respectively. These layers are separated by 34 layers of separator material (0.022 mm) alternating between individual sheets and a long sheet running through the entire jelly stack. The pouch casing is made of a 0.1 mm thick polymer-aluminum sheet. The coating area on the cathode and anode are 56 mm × 61 mm and 58 mm × 63 mm, respectively, leading to an overhang of 1 mm on each side of the anode.

The swelling-induced charging of the cell is characterized during their first commercial cycle. The cells are first discharged using a constant current, constant voltage (CC-CV) procedure at a C-rate of 0.5C, i.e. a constant current (CC) phase with a current of 2 A, down to a threshold voltage of 3 V, followed by a constant voltage (CV) phase at a tension of 3 V down to 10% of the C-rate, i.e. a current of 0.2 A. To limit the influence of the discharge process, a 2-h resting period is implemented before the charging experiments. The state of charge (SoC) is defined using a Coulomb counting method by dividing the capacity by the typical capacity, i.e. 4 Ah.

The thickness change of the pouch cells (i.e. swelling) is characterized over a single CC-CV charging step at C-rates of 0.5C, 1 C and 1.5C, corresponding to constant currents in the CC phase of 2 A, 4 A and 6 A, respectively. For the CC section, the cutoff voltage is set at the maximum voltage given by the manufacturer, i.e. 4.43 V. The current cutoff for the CV section is set at 10% of the initial current, i.e. 0.2 A, 0.4 A and 0.8 A. The swelling evolution is further monitored during a 15-min rest stage after the charging. For the unconstrained charging, (section 2.3), a following discharge is performed using a CC-CV step with C-rate identical as the charging step, a cutoff voltage of 3.0 V and a current cutoff for the CV section set at 10% of the initial current. The discharge is reported in Appendix A2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unconstrained charging with surround DIC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The surround DIC system proposed by Jordan et al. [35] is used to capture the spatial distribution of the thickness change of the pouch cell during charging. The setup consists of four 5 Mpx cameras equipped with 50 mm lenses, arranged in two pairs for stereo imaging (Fig. 1c). The camera pairs allow for data acquisition on both large faces of the cell (see Fig. 1b and c). Due to the limited change in the in-plane dimensions, the other faces are not considered in this study. To achieve optimal lighting conditions and reduce reflections on the cell surface, two blue LED lights are used. Details on the DIC system hardware are reported in Table 2 . During the experiments, the voltage and current are continuously monitored at 1 Hz, while images are acquired at a frequency of 0.05 Hz. The cell is mounted centrally between the camera systems, with the large faces perpendicular to the optical axis of the cameras in each system (Fig. 1c). To minimize the externally-applied constraints, the cell is connected via the power leads at the top and supported at the bottom (Fig. 1b). Since only gravity acts on the cell and may affect the results, these experiments are referred to as “unconstrained”. Data from each camera system is analyzed using VIC-3D 7 software. Table 3 presents the details of the DIC parameters. Each camera system provides surface meshes of the faces in a global coordinate system obtained through the calibration of the surround DIC system. The meshes are then aligned with the coordinate system defined in Fig. 1b using a Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno algorithm [35]. The origin of the coordinate system is set at the center of the cell. This allows monitoring the in-plane strain components on each cell surface and the out-of-plane motion. A ray-tracing algorithm further calculates the thickness evolution by measuring the Euclidean distance between the closest points on each cell face. A thickness field and a local thickness strain field are obtained by dividing the current thickness field by the initial thickness field. For the 0.5C C-rate, three experiments are performed. Due to the high repeatability observed and the limited number of cells available, only one experiment is performed at a C-rate of 1.0C as well as 1.5C.

The thickness change is further investigated under constant force with the same C-rates (0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C). For these experiments, a 250 kN electromechanical universal testing machine (Instron 5580) is used. The cell is constrained between two non-polished steel platens, one spherically seated and one equipped with three Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). No insulating layers are used between the pouch and the two platens. As the platens are significantly larger than the pouch cell and as the current leads are extended with electrical wire, this setup represents uniaxial compression of the entire pouch cell. The change in distance between the two platens is obtained as the average of the three LVDTs displacement measurements and used as a scalar measure of the thickness change. The strain is obtained by normalizing the thickness by the thickness after pre-compression. The rate of data acquisition for the force and LVDT-measurement displacement is 0.2 Hz while the voltage and current are recorded at a frequency of 1 Hz. Two force levels are considered, 4 kN and 0.1 kN. For an initially flat cell, these force levels correspond to the applied pressures of 1 MPa and 0.026 MPa. Prior to electrical cycling, the cells are compressed to the compression load level under displacement control at a rate of 0.3 mm/min. Two repetitions at each C-rates are performed for the 4 kN constant load, while a single experiment is performed at each C-rates for the 0.1 kN constant load.

Fig. 2 shows the spatial distribution of the initial thickness (first column) for three different cells. In addition, it depicts the swelling strain for three C-rates. The strain distribution is shown at increasing SoC (0.25, 0.5 and 0.75) as well as at the end of the charging. The initial thickness map includes traces of the position of the tapes that were used to hold the jelly stack during manufacturing, along with 4% thickness variation within each cell. For the slowest charging rate (0.5C, top row), the swelling is spatially homogeneous up to a state of charge of 0.5. At a SoC of 0.75, the strain localizes at the bottom of the cells, on the vertical sides as well as in the center. For the higher C-rates, the swelling strain field is already heterogeneous at a state of charge of 0.25. Higher strain values are observed on the edges of both cells as well as near the center for the C-rate of 1.5C. It should be noted that, at a 1 C charging rate, no localization peak is observed at the center. For the 1.5C charging, the maximum swelling is constant at a SoC of 0.75 and at the end of the charge. Away from the edges and the center, a plateau region with low and constant strain is observed. This plateau is identified using a RANSAC algorithm [36] with an inlier threshold corresponding to half of the strain standard deviation. The plateau strain is defined as the average of the inliers’ values. Homogeneous swelling is then defined when less than 50% of the points are inliers (i.e. within half a standard deviation of the fitted plane), while heterogeneous swelling is defined when more than 70% of the points are inliers. The transition from homogeneous to heterogeneous swelling starts after 4170 s (SoC of 0.58) when charging at 0.5C, after 220 s (SoC of about 0.06) when charging at 1 C, and after 320s (SoC of about 0.13) when charging at 1.5C. The transitions last for about 300 s (SoC of 0.04) for the three charging at 0.5C, 300 s (SoC of 0.09) for 1 C charging and 120 s (SoC of 0.05) for 1.5C charging.

To gain more insight into both spatial and temporal evolutions, Fig. 3 presents profile cuts in the horizontal (left) and vertical (right) directions across the center of the cell every 0.1 SoC for all C-rates. At mid-height (horizontal profile), the swelling strains exhibit a local maximum on each side of the cell and a local maximum at the center (except for the C-rate of 1.0C). The vertical profiles show a localization at the bottom of the cell as well as some localization at the top for the highest two C-rates. For the highest C-rate, the profile shows a higher swelling towards the bottom of the cell. The profile confirms the plateau strain definition, with heterogeneities occurring around a state of charge of 0.6, 0.1, and 0.1 for the C-rates of 0.5C, 1 C and 1.5C, respectively.

Fig. 4 shows the voltage and current evolution (a-b) as well as the strain (c-d) for all repetitions of the 0.5C charging as a function of time (left column) and state of charge (right column). For all experiments, the voltage rises rapidly at the beginning of charging, up to 3.95 V in less than 340 s (0.045 SoC). The voltage further rises to 4.06 V, in 0.4 h (around 0.2 SoC) and exhibits a slight plateau up to 0.9 h (around 0.4 SoC). After the plateau, the voltage increases in a convex manner to 4.43 V and transitions to constant voltage after 1.6 h (SoC 0.8, indicated by the cross). In the CV phase, the current decreases linearly with the state of charge and the charging process ends after 2.6 h at a SoC of 0.97. During the 15 min rest period, the voltage decreases by approximately 45 mV. Fig. 4c and d shows the evolution of the swelling strain as a function of time and state of charge for the minimum, maximum and plateau strains. For about 1.15 h (0.58 SoC), the strain evolves in a narrow band with less than 0.01 strain difference between the minimum and the maximum. This corresponds to the homogeneous swelling observed in Figs. 2 and 3 and correlates with the end of the voltage plateau. Initially, the swelling strain evolves in a concave manner followed by a convex part. Heterogenous swelling is characterized by the sudden increase in the maximum strain from about 0.025 to 0.08 in 0.5 h. During the same period, the plateau strain only increases by about 0.01. Soon after the CC to CV transition, the highest maximum strain is reached at a state of charge of 0.9 (and a current of 0.21C) and a decrease is observed down to a value of 0.075 at the end of the charge, while the minimum and plateau strain reaches strains of 0.033 and 0.037, respectively. Hence, the cell's edges and the central hotspot reach their maximum swelling strain first, while the rest of the cell continues swelling. During the rest phase, both plateau and minimum strains remain constant while the maximum strain reduces at the same rate as observed after the peak swelling. The strain response of the three cells is very similar with a strain variation below 0.005 at all times.

Fig. 5 shows the same evolution for all C-rates. The higher rates are characterized by a higher voltage at early stages, close to 4.3 V and a smaller CC period with a duration of 0.31 h, corresponding to 0.3 SoC and 0.5 SoC for the C-rates of 1.0C and 1.5C, respectively. In the case of the 1.5C charge, the voltage exhibits a slight overshoot at 4.36 V. During the CV phase, the currents decrease at a higher rate with increasing C-rates. Overall, the charging time decreases with an increasing C-rate from 2.62 h to 1.71 h and 0.99 h with a final SoC of 0.97, 0.92 and 0.92. The strain evolution (Fig. 5c and d) for the 1C charging shows a rapid increase of the plateau strain and no visible transition from homogeneous to heterogeneous swelling. The maximum strain rises almost linearly through time (and SoC) until reaching a peak value of 0.092 after 1.25 h (SoC of 0.89) and stays constant until the end of charge. The maximum strain then decreases during the rest phase by 0.005. Both plateau and minimum strains follow a similar evolution with peaks at 0.038 and 0.023 but do not exhibit a decrease during the rest phase. It is remarkable that the plateau strain evolution with the SoC perfectly coincides for the charging at 0.5C and 1.0C. The strain for the 1 C charging shows a larger spread with both lower minimum values (by about 0.012) and higher maximum values (up to 0.034 at a SoC of 0.5). For the highest C-rate (i.e. 1.5C), a homogeneous swelling is observed up to a SoC of 0.13, where the maximum strain increases rapidly. The plateau strain strongly increases after the CC to CV transition. All the strain values go through a peak which is first reached by the maximum strain (0.109 at SoC of 0.86), then by the plateau strain (0.066 at SoC of 0.89) and finally by the minimum strain (0.047 at SoC of 0.90). During the rest phase, the strain decreases by about 0.01. Looking at the evolution with state of charge, the plateau strain is similar to the other two charge rates up to a SoC around 0.4. Then the plateau strain and the minimum strain become larger than the plateau strain of the other two C-rates.

Table 4 includes the highest strain values observed for all C-rates under unconstrained charging. The slowest C-rate (0.5C) shows the most homogeneous swelling profile with a maximum to minimum difference of 0.048. This value increases to 0.069 for the C-rate of 1.0C and to 0.062 for the C-rate of 1.5C. The plateau and mean values for the 0.5C and 1.0C charging are similar (0.04, and 0.046, respectively) and increases to 0.066 and 0.072 (about 60% higher) for the 1.5C charging. It should be noted that the spatial average of the strain closely follows the plateau strain evolution and separates at the onset of heterogeneous swelling, due to increasing strain in the localized regions. The in-plane strains maps (not shown) show more scatter and are close to the noise level of the DIC. Due to the larger swelling on the cell edges, bending of the layers occurs. In turn, the in-plane strains exhibit positive values on the outside, and becomes negative towards the center.

Figs. 6 and 7
show the voltage, current and strain evolutions during constrained charging under a constant load of 4 kN (Figs. 6) and 0.1 kN (Fig. 7), for C-rates of 0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C. In this setup, the swelling is measured as the average displacement of three LVDTs and therefore a single scalar value is reported.

Under a constant load of 4 kN (Fig. 6), the voltage evolution shows similar feature to the unconstrained swelling case. For a C-rate of 0.5C, the voltage rises first to about 4 V, then to about 4.16 V before exhibiting a small plateau (between SoC of 0.2 and 0.5). The transition from CC to CV occurs after 1.5 h (SoC of 0.77) and the end of charge occurs after 2.47 h (SoC of 0.94). During the CV phase, the voltage decreases linearly with the state of charge. For the 1.0C (resp. 1.5C) charging, the voltage rises to about 4.33 V (resp. 4.43 V) within less than 3 min, then exhibits a slight overshoot and transitions from CC to CV at 0.48 h and SoC of 0.49 (resp. 0.15 and SoC of 0.23). A slight plateau in voltage is observed for the 1.0C charging. The current in the CV phase decreases similarly for all C-rates. During the rest phase, the voltage decreases to 4.42 V, 4.40 V and 4.39 V for the 0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C charging, respectively. For all cells, the strain increases with state of charge, exhibits a maximum during the CV phase and decreases during the end of charge and the rest period. Up to a SoC of 0.1, the strain evolves similarly for all C-rates. For the C-rate of 0.5C, the strain continues increasing and reaches a plateau between a SoC of 0.25 and a SoC between around 0.40 for both cells. Then, the strain increases more rapidly up to the CC to CV transition. In the CV stage, the strain evolution becomes concave and reaches a maximum value of 0.042 at a SoC of 0.88. During the rest phase, the strain decreases by about 0.0025. For the C-rate of 1.0C, the strain evolves almost linearly up to a strain value of 0.053 at a SoC of 0.65 and reaches a maximum of 0.064 at SoC of 0.87. The strain decreases by 0.0021 during the remaining charge and by 0.0037 during the rest phase. The evolution is similar for the C-rate of 1.5C, with a maximum value of 0.066 and 0.0729 reached at a SoC of 0.83 and 0.92 and a further decrease by about 0.001 during the end of the charging and 0.005 during the rest phase. The main strain values are reported in Table 4.

Under a constant load of 0.1 kN (Fig. 7), the same trends can be observed and are summarized in Table 4. A voltage plateau is visible between SoC 0.25 and 0.4 for the C-rate of 0.5C and a smaller plateau is also observed for the C-rate of 1.0C between states of charge of 0.12 and 0.25. The CC-CV transition is observed after 1.60 h (resp. 0.54 h) at a SoC of 0.80 (resp. 0.55) for the C-rates 0.5C and 1.0C. For the highest C-rate, the voltage rises to 4.45 V in less than 2 min. The current then drops to 1.33C before increasing back to 1.4C after 0.1 h. Following this event, all currents show a similar evolution with time and state of charge. The charge ends after 2.46 h, 1.48 h and 1.20 h, at a SoC of 0.96, 0.92 and 0.088, for C-rates of 0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C. The strain evolution with state of charge is similar for all cells up to a SoC of 0.0615 when the 1.5C charge starts deviating, followed by the 1.0C charging at a SoC of 0.011. The strain evolves linearly with a slope of 0.175/SoC and 0.190/SoC for the C-rates of 1.0C and 1.5C. At a SoC of 0.3, the strain evolution for the C-rate 0.5C has a strong inflection and evolves almost linearly with a slope of 0.134/SoC. As for the 4 kN load, for all C-rates, the strain exhibits a maximum during the CV phase followed by a decrease until the end of the rest phase. The maximum strains are 0.080, 0.105 and 0.108 reached at a time of 1.93 h, 0.94 h and 0.85 h (SoC of 0.92, 0.81 and 0.81) for the C-rates of 0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C. The strain then decreases by 0.007, 0.013 and 0.010 by the end of the rest phase.

Fig. 8 shows the voltage, current (left column) and strain (right column) evolutions for each C-rates and for all charging scenarios. For the slowest C-rate (Fig. 8a), all voltage and current evolutions are very similar with a slightly lower voltage for the unconstrained charging. Up to a SoC of 0.3, the strain (Fig. 8b) evolves similarly with the constrained charging strains close to the minimum strain observed in the unconstrained case. The strain for the 0.1 kN constrained charging then rapidly increases, reaching higher values than the maximum values of the unconstrained charging. After the CC-CV transition, the maximum of the unconstrained charging reaches similar values, and the peak strain and further decay are identical. The 4 kN constrained charging closely follows the evolution of the plateau strain with the smaller flat region and a peak strain reached at SoC of 0.88. As for the C-rate of 0.5C, the strain for all charging scenarios is initially the same.

For the C-rate of 1.0C (Fig. 8c and d), the two constrained charging exhibits a very similar voltage evolution characterized by a small overshoot. On the other hand, the unconstrained charging transitions from CC to CV much earlier and exhibits a slower current decrease during the CV phase. As for the C-rate of 0.5C, the strain for all charging scenarios is initially the same At a SoC of 0.12, the strain for the 4.0 kN load constrained charging rises above the plateau strain, and the strain for the 0.1 kN constrained charging rises above the maximum strain. It should be noted that the maximum strain from the unconstrained case as well as the two constrained charging strains reaches a peak at a state of charge around 0.8.

For the highest C-rate (1.5C, Fig. 8e and f), the voltage and current evolution are similar for the two constrained charging cases, while the unconstrained charging exhibits an almost twice as long CC phase, combined with a faster decrease of the current in the CV phase. The strain evolution deviates right from the beginning, with the constrained charging cases exhibiting a higher strain evolution with state of charge. Similarly to the C-rate of 1.0C, the 0.1 kN load constrained charging exhibits the same swelling behavior as the maximum value in the unconstrained charging, while the 4 kN load constrained charging stays close to the plateau strain.

The observed large swelling strain, its heterogeneous nature and the relaxation during the CV and rest phases correlate with lithium plating as the main large swelling mechanism (e.g. Refs. [21,22,29,30]). Using the surround DIC setup provides crucial insight by allowing to obtain a precise thickness evolution map. In comparison with previous pointwise measurements (e.g. Refs. [21,37]), surround DIC allows capturing the displacement map on the two large surfaces of the cell, enabling the creation of surface strain and swelling maps. In contrast to single-face DIC, monitoring the displacement of two opposite surfaces removes possible rigid body rotations and bending effects which would affect the swelling and in-plane strain maps. On the other hand, thickness evolution using laser profilometry (e.g. Refs. [29–31]) is based on two-face measurement but does not enable in-plane strain mapping and exhibit a limited temporal frequency as it monitors the cell thickness a single line at a time, requiring a few seconds to travel though the entire cell. Surround-DIC therefore enables both high spatial and temporal resolution monitoring of the thickness evolution and surface strains during charging and is particularly suitable for fast-charging scenarios.

In this study, surround DIC resolves the swelling spatial heterogeneity and temporal evolution. Following the literature associating large swelling and thickness overshoot with lithium plating (e.g Refs. [21,22,29–31,37]), it enables measuring extent of lithium plating and comparing the plating-induced swelling with the intercalation-induced swelling. Besides lithium plating, the observed swelling could originate from internal delamination or gas evolution. Furthermore, as only the external surfaces of the cell are observed, it is not possible to resolve the possible through-thickness swelling heterogeneities.

It is assumed that all material points undergoing lithium plating will exhibit a decrease in swelling strain towards the end of the charging due to lithium stripping. This follows the experimental evidence (e.g. Refs. [21,22,29–31,37]) and the modeling approach proposed in Ref. [37]. Figure A1 (Appendix) presents the strain relaxation fields for unconstrained charging at a C-rate of 0.5C, 1 C and 1.5C. The relaxation is computed as the difference between the maximum strain reached during charging (at each spatial location) and the strain at the end of the rest phase. For the lowest C-rate, regions exhibiting large relaxation are observed at the bottom of cell as well as in the center. For the C-rate of 1 C, only the lowest two corners exhibit relaxation. For both cases, the relaxation occurs in the region of maximum swelling. Finally for the fastest charge, the entire cell exhibits large relaxation (above 0.006). Thus, at a C-rate of 1.5C, lithium plating is observed on the entire cell surface, with a more pronounced swelling/plating on the edges. For increasing C-rates, 16%, 12% and 100% of the cell surface exhibit a strain decrease of more than 0.003. For the C-rates of 0.5C and 1.0C, where the majority of the cell do not exhibit strain overshoot, the plateau strain can be assumed to represent the swelling strain with limited (or no) lithium-plating (referred to as intercalation-dominated). This differentiation would not be feasible with a single point measurement. With a similar evolution for the C-rates of 0.5C and 1.0C, the intercalation-induced swelling can be assumed independent of the C-rate. The plateau strain exhibits the conventional S-shape with a lower swelling rate between SoC of 0.25 and 0.5. The plateau strain for the 1.5C charging coincides with the one for lower C-rates up to a SoC of 0.4. Therefore, this state of charge can be related to the presence of lithium plating on the entire cell's surface. This observation could point towards to the onset of plating occurring around the phase transition from LiC36 to LiC12 [38]. It is also noteworthy that all swelling curves coincide at a low state of charge independently of the C-rate (Figs. 5–8) and of the charging method (Fig. 8), i.e. in the absence of lithium plating. Although excellent repeatability of the swelling strain is observed during 0.5C charging, the absence of a large swelling strain region in the center of the cell for the 1 C charging rate indicates possible experimental scatter and warrants further investigation into this phenomenon.

When discussing the constrained charging, it is important to note that the observed strain is the sum of the mechanical strain and the swelling strain. The mechanical strain is dependent on the applied load as well as the contact area between the platen and the cell. This hinders the direct evaluation of the swelling strain as the contact area might change due to swelling heterogeneities. The following observations are made with this limitation in mind and consider that the contact area remains close to the total area of the cell, i.e. the mechanical strain remains almost constant during the charging process. This is further supported by the convex stress strain relationship of the cell. Comparing the unconstrained charging with the constrained charging at 0.5C, the following observations can be made: applying load of 4 kN significantly reduces the amount of lithium plating as the observed swelling strain is close to the plateau strain. Furthermore, at the end of the rest phase, i.e. after lithium stripping, the swelling strain reaches the plateau strain. Applying a load of 0.1 kN leads to earlier plating with an onset of plating occurring at a SoC of 0.25 but the total plating magnitude is similar to the one observed in the unconstrained case. At a C-rate of 1.0C, applying a load of 4 kN decreases the amount of plating compared to the 0.1 kN load but is not as successful as for the lower C-rate. This is also true for a C-rate of 1.5C, but leads to a swelling closer to the plateau strain observed during unconstrained charging.

It should be noted that for all C-rates, the difference between the strain under 0.1 kN and 4 kN load is initially constant and then evolves linearly with a slope which is almost independent of the C-rate. This indicates that the swelling strain under constrained compression in the plating regime can be additively decomposed in a state of charge dependent part and a C-rate dependent part, i.e.
ε s
(
S o C , C − rate , F
)
= f
(
S o C , F
)
+ g
(
C − rate
)
, where
ε s
denotes the swelling strain and
F
the applied load.

This study examined the charging-induced swelling behavior of small-format lithium-ion pouch cells during their first commercial cycle under varying rates of charge and mechanical constraints. Unconstrained charging experiments, monitored using surround three-dimensional digital image correlation, provided full-field measurements of in-plane strain and the thickness strain, while constrained charging was characterized using extensometer-based thickness measurements under constant compressive load.

The results demonstrate a strong dependence of the maximum swelling strain and subsequent relaxation on the C-rate, as well as on the state of charge and applied mechanical constraint. For all charging conditions, the largest swelling was observed during the constant-voltage phase, followed by a partial strain relaxation during the CV phase and subsequent rest period. Full-field thickness measurements reveal pronounced spatial heterogeneity in swelling, with localized strain concentrations developing preferentially at the cell edges and in a central region. By separating low-variance plateau strain from localized maximum strain, and by tracking strain relaxation, surround DIC facilitates a distinction between intercalation-dominated swelling and plating-associated contributions. Comparison with constrained charging experiments shows that the maximum strain measured during unconstrained charging closely corresponds to the response under low compressive load (0.1 kN), whereas the plateau strain matches the swelling observed under higher compression (4 kN). Despite the intrinsic limitation of the constrained experiments in measuring the swelling strain, this correspondence suggests that increasing applied pressure might lead to progressive suppression of lithium plating. At the lowest C-rate, the plateau strain measured by surround DIC is nearly identical to the swelling strain observed under 4 kN compression, suggesting that moderate external pressure is sufficient to limit plating-induced deformation in this regime. Furthermore, comparison between the two constraint levels reveals that the difference in swelling strain evolves independently of the rate of charge. This observation supports an additive decomposition of the swelling response in the plating regime into a state-of-charge- and force-dependent component and a rate-of-charge-dependent component.

Overall, this work demonstrates that surround digital image correlation provides a powerful experimental tool to resolve the spatial extent and temporal evolution of charging-induced swelling in lithium-ion pouch cells. The ability to distinguish heterogeneous plating-induced deformation characterized by expansion overshoot from homogeneous intercalation-driven swelling offers new opportunities for validating electro-chemo-mechanical models and for guiding the mechanical design and operating strategies of pouch-cell-based battery systems. Follow-up studies should focus on linking the information obtained from surround-DIC over a larger number of cycles to long-term electrochemical performance degradation.

Christoph Pirker: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Dirk Mohr: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Thomas Tancogne-Dejean: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Mr. Benoit Jordan, Mr. Gabriel Hayoun, for their help in gathering data using the Surround Digital Image Correlation System and to Mr. Julian Heidenreich for valuable discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: To gain more insight into the possible permanent deformation of the cell, surround DIC is used to monitor the discharge of three cells, at C-rate of 0.5C, 1 C and 1.5C. The discharge is performed using a CC-CV step with C-rate identical as the charging step. Figure A2 shows discharge cycle for the 1.5C discharge and features the voltage and current evolution (a-b) as well as the strain (c-d) as a function of time (left column) and state of charge (right column). The charging cycle is shown for completeness. During discharge, the voltage decreases rapidly, down to 3.64 V in less than 140 s (0.047 SoC). Following this quick evolution, the voltage decreases almost linearly, both in time and SoC and reaches the cutoff value of 3.0 V after 0.57 h (at a Soc of 0.07). A faster voltage evolution is observed for SoC lower than 0.2. Finally, the CV phase lasts 220 s and leads to a final SoC of 0.02.

Figure A2c-d shows the evolution of the swelling strain as a function of time and state of charge for the minimum, maximum and plateau strains. During the entire discharge period, the swelling strain decreases, in an almost linear manner at a rate of 0.051/h (0.032/SoC) for the plateau, minimum and maximum values. At the end of the discharge, the strain decreased by approximately 0.032 everywhere on the cell, leading to permanent swelling of 0.007, 0.018 and 0.068 for the minimum, plateau and maximum location.

Figure A2e shows the spatial distribution of the strain from the beginning to the end of the discharge. The maximum strain is observed along the edges of the cell as well as in the center (see Fig. 2), with no significant variation of the strain distribution, further indicating a uniform decrease in swelling strain during discharge.

Figure A3 shows the same evolution for all C-rates. The voltage evolves rapidly to 3.78 V and 4.13 V for the 1 C and 0.5C discharge, higher than the one observed for the highest C-rate. Subsequently, the voltage evolves in a similar fashion for all C-rate, with an almost linear voltage decrease at a rate of 0.7/SoC and a faster evolution for SoC below 0.2. Note that the voltage was not recorded until the end of the discharge cycle and that the evolution below a SoC of 0.07 cannot be directly observed. The strain decreases (Fig. A3c-d) in a similar manner at a rate of 0.032/SoC for all cells and location (i.e. minimum, plateau and maximum) as a function of the state of charge. As for the charging, the plateau strain evolution for the lowest two C-rate is identical. The plateau strain for the highest C-rate shows a relaxation during the charge CV phase as well as during the rest phase. However, during the discharge phase, about the same amount of strain is recovered independently of the C-rate. This supports the conclusion that a single swelling mechanism acts during the discharge process, independently of the C-rate and that lithium stripping mostly occurs during the charging CV phase and the rest phase.

Figure A4 shows a photograph of a selected anode after discharge. Note that the cell has been stored in a discharged state for a long period of time and that no direct traces of lithium plating are observed. Nonetheless, a spatial color gradient is observed with a brown color next to the anode overhang and in the cell center. This directly corresponds to the highly-swollen zones in Fig. 2 and A2 and could be tentatively related to SEI thickening during storage in regions where plating occurred during charging.
Fig. A1
Supplementary figure showing strain relaxation field for the unconstrained charging at various C-rates. From left to right: 0.5C, 1.0C and 1.5C.
Fig. A2
Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution observed during charging and discharging using surround DIC with a C-rate of 1.5C. The evolution is presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. The crosses denote the change from CC to CV while the circles indicate the end of charge. The beginning of discharge is denoted with a square symbol. The charging current is shown in grey. (e) The strain maps, from left to right, at the end of the rest phase and at decreasing State of Charge (0.75, 0.5, 0.25, 0.02), with thickness strain displayed). Note that the cell terminals are located at the top of the cell.
Fig. A3
Voltage (top row) and strain (bottom) evolution observed using surround DIC during charging and discharging presented as a function of (a, c) time and (b, d) state of charge. All strains including minimum, maximum and plateau strains. The end of the charging phase and of the rest phase are reported with crosses and full lines. In (d), the colored patches are only shown for the discharge phase.
Fig. A4
Photograph of a selected anode after discharging and long-term storage. Next to the dark overhang edges, a different color can be seen in the can be seen as a trace of heterogeneous swelling. The same color appears at the center of the cell and correlates with the high swelling regions in Fig. A2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MLESAC: a new robust estimator with application to estimating image geometry
Comput. Vis. Image Underst.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Torr PHS, Zisserman A. MLESAC: A New Robust Estimator with Application to Estimating Image Geometry. Comput Vis Image Underst 2000;78:138-156. https://doi.org/10.1006/CVIU.1999.0832.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium plating induced volume expansion overshoot of lithium-ion batteries: experimental analysis and modeling
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang S, Ren D, Xu C, Han X, Liu X, Lu L, et al. Lithium plating induced volume expansion overshoot of lithium-ion batteries: Experimental analysis and modeling. J Power Sources 2024;593:233946. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.JPOWSOUR.2023.233946.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hysteresis and current dependence of the thickness change of lithium-ion cells with graphite anode
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Grimsmann F, Brauchle F, Gerbert T, Gruhle A, Knipper M, Parisi J. Hysteresis and current dependence of the thickness change of lithium-ion cells with graphite anode. J Energy Storage 2017;12:132-137. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.EST.2017.04.006.
